(denoted as *R*🜨 or *R*E) is the distance from the center of [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth") to a point on or near its surface. Approximating the [figure of Earth](/wiki/Figure_of_Earth "Figure of Earth") by an [Earth spheroid](/wiki/Earth_spheroid "Earth spheroid") (an [oblate ellipsoid](/wiki/Oblate_ellipsoid "Oblate ellipsoid")), the radius ranges from a maximum (**equatorial radius**, denoted *a*) of about 6,378 km (3,963 mi) to a minimum (**polar radius**, denoted *b*) of nearly 6,357 km (3,950 mi).

**Earth radius** (denoted as *R*🜨 or *R*E) is the distance from the center of [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth") to a point on or near its surface. Approximating the [figure of Earth](/wiki/Figure_of_Earth "Figure of Earth") by an [Earth spheroid](/wiki/Earth_spheroid "Earth spheroid") (an [oblate ellipsoid](/wiki/Oblate_ellipsoid "Oblate ellipsoid")), the radius ranges from a maximum (**equatorial radius**, denoted *a*) of about 6,378 km (3,963 mi) to a minimum (**polar radius**, denoted *b*) of nearly 6,357 km (3,950 mi).

A globally-average value is usually considered to be 6,371 kilometres (3,959 mi) with a 0.3% variability (±10 km) for the following reasons.
The [International Union of Geodesy and Geophysics](/wiki/International_Union_of_Geodesy_and_Geophysics "International Union of Geodesy and Geophysics") (IUGG) provides three reference values: the *mean radius* (*R*1) of three radii measured at two equator points and a pole; the *authalic radius*, which is the radius of a sphere with the same surface area (*R*2); and the *volumetric radius*, which is the radius of a sphere having the same volume as the ellipsoid (*R*3).[[2]](#cite_note-Moritz-2) All three values are about 6,371 kilometres (3,959 mi).

Other ways to define and measure the Earth's radius involve either the spheroid's [radius of curvature](/wiki/Radius_of_curvature "Radius of curvature") or the actual [topography](/wiki/Topography "Topography"). A few definitions yield values outside the range between the [polar](/wiki/Geographical_pole "Geographical pole") radius and [equatorial](/wiki/Equator "Equator") radius because they account for localized effects.

A *nominal Earth radius* (denoted ![{\displaystyle {\mathcal {R}}_{\mathrm {E} }^{\mathrm {N} }}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/094e54a9094cace8bcae1656a510becd421f1481)) is sometimes used as a [unit of measurement](/wiki/Unit_of_measurement "Unit of measurement") in [astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy") and [geophysics](/wiki/Geophysics "Geophysics"), a [conversion factor](/wiki/Conversion_factor "Conversion factor") used when expressing planetary properties as multiples or fractions of a constant terrestrial radius; if the choice between equatorial or polar radii is not explicit, the equatorial radius is to be assumed, as recommended by the [International Astronomical Union](/wiki/International_Astronomical_Union "International Astronomical Union") (IAU).[[1]](#cite_note-IAU_XXIX-1)

## Introduction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_radius&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Introduction")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Earth_oblateness_to_scale.svg/330px-Earth_oblateness_to_scale.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Earth_oblateness_to_scale.svg)

A scale diagram of the [oblateness](/wiki/Flattening "Flattening") of the 2003 [IERS](/wiki/IERS "IERS") [reference ellipsoid](/wiki/Reference_ellipsoid "Reference ellipsoid"), with north at the top. The light blue region is a circle. The outer edge of the dark blue line is an [ellipse](/wiki/Ellipse "Ellipse") with the same [minor axis](/wiki/Minor_axis "Minor axis") as the circle and the same [eccentricity](/wiki/Eccentricity_(mathematics)#Ellipses "Eccentricity (mathematics)") as the Earth. The red line represents the [Karman line](/wiki/Karman_line "Karman line") 100 km (62 mi) above [sea level](/wiki/Sea_level "Sea level"), while the yellow area denotes the [altitude](/wiki/Apsis "Apsis") range of the [ISS](/wiki/International_Space_Station "International Space Station") in [low Earth orbit](/wiki/Low_Earth_orbit "Low Earth orbit").

Main articles: [Figure of the Earth](/wiki/Figure_of_the_Earth "Figure of the Earth"), [Earth ellipsoid](/wiki/Earth_ellipsoid "Earth ellipsoid"), and [Reference ellipsoid](/wiki/Reference_ellipsoid "Reference ellipsoid")

[Earth's rotation](/wiki/Earth%27s_rotation "Earth's rotation"), internal density variations, and external [tidal forces](/wiki/Tidal_force "Tidal force") cause its shape to deviate systematically from a perfect sphere.[[a]](#cite_note-3) Local [topography](/wiki/Topography "Topography") increases the variance, resulting in a surface of profound complexity. Our descriptions of Earth's surface must be simpler than reality in order to be tractable. Hence, we create models to approximate characteristics of Earth's surface, generally relying on the simplest model that suits the need.

Each of the models in common use involve some notion of the geometric [radius](/wiki/Radius "Radius"). Strictly speaking, spheres are the only solids to have radii, but broader uses of the term *radius* are common in many fields, including those dealing with models of Earth. The following is a partial list of models of Earth's surface, ordered from exact to more approximate:

* The actual surface of Earth
* The [geoid](/wiki/Geoid "Geoid"), defined by [mean sea level](/wiki/Mean_sea_level "Mean sea level") at each point on the real surface[[b]](#cite_note-4)
* A [spheroid](/wiki/Spheroid "Spheroid"), also called an [ellipsoid](/wiki/Ellipsoid "Ellipsoid") of revolution, [geocentric](/wiki/Geodetic_system#Geodetic_versus_geocentric_latitude "Geodetic system") to model the entire Earth, or else [geodetic](/wiki/Geodetic_system#Geodetic_versus_geocentric_latitude "Geodetic system") for regional work[[c]](#cite_note-5)
* A [sphere](/wiki/Sphere "Sphere")

In the case of the geoid and ellipsoids, the fixed distance from any point on the model to the specified center is called *"a radius of the Earth"* or *"the radius of the Earth at that point"*.[[d]](#cite_note-6) It is also common to refer to any *[mean radius](#Mean_radii)* of a spherical model as *"the radius of the earth"*. When considering the Earth's real surface, on the other hand, it is uncommon to refer to a "radius", since there is generally no practical need. Rather, elevation above or below sea level is useful.

Regardless of the model, any of these *geocentric* radii falls between the polar minimum of about 6,357 km and the equatorial maximum of about 6,378 km (3,950 to 3,963 mi). Hence, the Earth deviates from a perfect sphere by only a third of a percent, which supports the spherical model in most contexts and justifies the term "radius of the Earth". While specific values differ, the concepts in this article generalize to any major [planet](/wiki/Planet "Planet").

### Physics of Earth's deformation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_radius&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Physics of Earth's deformation")]

Further information: [Equatorial bulge](/wiki/Equatorial_bulge "Equatorial bulge")

Rotation of a planet causes it to approximate an *[oblate ellipsoid](/wiki/Spheroid "Spheroid")/spheroid* with a bulge at the [equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator") and flattening at the [North](/wiki/North_Pole "North Pole") and [South Poles](/wiki/South_Pole "South Pole"), so that the *equatorial radius* a is larger than the *polar radius* b by approximately aq. The *oblateness constant* q is given by

:   ![{\displaystyle q={\frac {a^{3}\omega ^{2}}{GM}},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/318f3ef055d946153757cae6dd8d2a1a1d88f0df)

where ω is the [angular frequency](/wiki/Angular_frequency "Angular frequency"), G is the [gravitational constant](/wiki/Gravitational_constant "Gravitational constant"), and M is the mass of the planet.[[e]](#cite_note-8) For the Earth ⁠1/*q*⁠ ≈ 289, which is close to the measured inverse [flattening](/wiki/Flattening "Flattening") ⁠1/*f*⁠ ≈ 298.257. Additionally, the bulge at the equator shows slow variations. The bulge had been decreasing, but since 1998 the bulge has increased, possibly due to redistribution of ocean mass via currents.[[4]](#cite_note-9)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Lowresgeoidheight.jpg/500px-Lowresgeoidheight.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lowresgeoidheight.jpg)

The variation in [density](/wiki/Density "Density") and [crustal](/wiki/Crust_(geology) "Crust (geology)") thickness causes gravity to vary across the surface and in time, so that the mean sea level differs from the ellipsoid. This difference is the *[geoid](/wiki/Geoid "Geoid") height*, positive above or outside the ellipsoid, negative below or inside. The geoid height variation is under 110 m (360 ft) on Earth. The geoid height can change abruptly due to earthquakes (such as the [Sumatra-Andaman earthquake](/wiki/2004_Indian_Ocean_earthquake "2004 Indian Ocean earthquake")) or reduction in ice masses (such as [Greenland](/wiki/Greenland "Greenland")).[[5]](#cite_note-10)

Not all deformations originate within the Earth. Gravitational attraction from the Moon or Sun can cause the Earth's surface at a given point to vary by tenths of a meter over a nearly 12-hour period (see [Earth tide](/wiki/Earth_tide "Earth tide")).

### Radius and local conditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_radius&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Radius and local conditions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Abu_Reyhan_Biruni-Earth_Circumference.svg/250px-Abu_Reyhan_Biruni-Earth_Circumference.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Abu_Reyhan_Biruni-Earth_Circumference.svg)

[Al-Biruni](/wiki/Al-Biruni#Geodesy_and_geography "Al-Biruni")'s (973 – c. 1050) method for calculation of the Earth's radius simplified measuring the circumference compared to taking measurements from two locations distant from each other.

Given local and transient influences on surface height, the values defined below are based on a "general purpose" model, refined as globally precisely as possible within 5 m (16 ft) of reference ellipsoid height, and to within 100 m (330 ft) of mean sea level (neglecting geoid height).

Additionally, the radius can be estimated from the curvature of the Earth at a point. Like a [torus](/wiki/Torus "Torus"), the curvature at a point will be greatest (tightest) in one direction (north–south on Earth) and smallest (flattest) perpendicularly (east–west). The corresponding [radius of curvature](/wiki/Radius_of_curvature_(applications) "Radius of curvature (applications)") depends on the location and direction of measurement from that point. A consequence is that a distance to the [true horizon](/wiki/Horizon "Horizon") at the equator is slightly shorter in the north–south direction than in the east–west direction.

In summary, local variations in terrain prevent defining a single "precise" radius. One can only adopt an idealized model. Since the estimate by [Eratosthenes](/wiki/Eratosthenes "Eratosthenes"), many models have been created. Historically, these models were based on regional topography, giving the best [reference ellipsoid](/wiki/Figure_of_the_Earth#Historical_Earth_ellipsoids "Figure of the Earth") for the area under survey. As satellite [remote sensing](/wiki/Remote_sensing "Remote sensing") and especially the [Global Positioning System](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System") gained importance, true global models were developed which, while not as accurate for regional work, best approximate the Earth as a whole.

## Extrema: equatorial and polar radii

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_radius&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Extrema: equatorial and polar radii")]

The following radii are derived from the [World Geodetic System](/wiki/World_Geodetic_System "World Geodetic System") 1984 ([WGS-84](/wiki/WGS-84 "WGS-84")) [reference ellipsoid](/wiki/Reference_ellipsoid "Reference ellipsoid").[[6]](#cite_note-tr8350_2-11) It is an idealized surface, and the Earth measurements used to calculate it have an uncertainty of ±2 m in both the equatorial and polar dimensions.[[7]](#cite_note-NGA-12) Additional discrepancies caused by topographical variation at specific locations can be significant. When identifying the position of an observable location, the use of more precise values for WGS-84 radii may not yield a corresponding improvement in [accuracy](/wiki/Accuracy "Accuracy").[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

The value for the equatorial radius is defined to the nearest 0.1 m in WGS-84. The value for the polar radius in this section has been rounded to the nearest 0.1 m, which is expected to be adequate for most uses. Refer to the WGS-84 ellipsoid if a more precise value for its polar radius is needed.

* The Earth's *equatorial radius* a, or [semi-major axis](/wiki/Semi-major_axis "Semi-major axis"),[[8]](#cite_note-Snyder_manual-13): 11 is the distance from its center to the [equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator") and equals 6,378.1370 km (3,963.1906 mi).[[9]](#cite_note-14) The equatorial radius is often used to compare Earth with other [planets](/wiki/Planet#Attributes "Planet").
* The Earth's *polar radius* b, or [semi-minor axis](/wiki/Semi-minor_axis "Semi-minor axis")[[8]](#cite_note-Snyder_manual-13): 11 is the distance from its center to the North and South Poles, and equals 6,356.7523 km (3,949.9028 mi).

## Location-dependent radii

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_radius&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Location-dependent radii")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Various_radii_vs._latitude.svg/250px-Various_radii_vs._latitude.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Various_radii_vs._latitude.svg)

Various radii as a function of latitude.

### Geocentric radius

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_radius&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Geocentric radius")]

Not to be confused with [Geocentric distance](/wiki/Geocentric_distance "Geocentric distance").

The *geocentric radius* is the distance from the Earth's center to a point on the spheroid surface at [geodetic latitude](/wiki/Geodetic_latitude "Geodetic latitude") φ, given by the formula
[[10]](#cite_note-15)

:   ![{\displaystyle R(\varphi )={\sqrt {\frac {(a^{2}\cos \varphi )^{2}+(b^{2}\sin \varphi )^{2}}{(a\cos \varphi )^{2}+(b\sin \varphi )^{2}}}},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c64f616bd6c63c20fed6252ba7934262d2f16a09)

where a and b are, respectively, the equatorial radius and the polar radius.

The maximum and minimum geocentric radii on the ellipsoid coincide, respectively, with the equatorial and polar radii, at the [vertices](/wiki/Vertex_(curve) "Vertex (curve)") of the ellipse.
However, the maximum and minimum radii of curvature coincide with the poles and Equator, in opposite regions than the geocentric radii, due to Earth's oblateness.

### Radii of curvature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_radius&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Radii of curvature")]

See also: [Spheroid § Curvature](/wiki/Spheroid#Curvature "Spheroid")

#### Principal radii of curvature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_radius&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Principal radii of curvature")]

There are two [principal radii of curvature](/wiki/Principal_curvature "Principal curvature"): along the meridional and prime-vertical [normal sections](/wiki/Normal_section "Normal section").

| Derivation |
| --- |
| The principal curvatures are the roots of Equation (125) in:[[11]](#cite_note-Lass-16)  {\displaystyle (EG-F^{2})\kappa ^{2}-(eG+gE-2fF)\kappa +(eg-f^{2})=0=\det(A-\kappa B),}  where in the [first fundamental form](/wiki/First_fundamental_form "First fundamental form") for a surface (Equation (112) in[[11]](#cite_note-Lass-16)):  {\displaystyle ds^{2}=\sum _{ij}a_{ij}dw^{i}dw^{j}=E\,d\varphi ^{2}+2F\,d\varphi \,d\lambda +G\,d\lambda ^{2},}  *E*, *F*, and *G* are elements of the [metric tensor](/wiki/Metric_tensor "Metric tensor"):  {\displaystyle A=a_{ij}=\sum _{\nu }{\frac {\partial r^{\nu }}{\partial w^{i}}}{\frac {\partial r^{\nu }}{\partial w^{j}}}={\begin{bmatrix}E&F\\F&G\end{bmatrix}},}  {\displaystyle r=[r^{1},r^{2},r^{3}]^{T}=[x,y,z]^{T}}, {\displaystyle w^{1}=\varphi }, {\displaystyle w^{2}=\lambda ,}  in the [second fundamental form](/wiki/Second_fundamental_form "Second fundamental form") for a surface (Equation (123) in[[11]](#cite_note-Lass-16)):  {\displaystyle 2D=\sum _{ij}b_{ij}dw^{i}dw^{j}=e\,d\varphi ^{2}+2f\,d\varphi \,d\lambda +g\,d\lambda ^{2},}  *e*, *f*, and *g* are elements of the shape tensor:  {\displaystyle B=b_{ij}=\sum _{\nu }n^{\nu }{\frac {\partial ^{2}r^{\nu }}{\partial w^{i}\partial w^{j}}}={\begin{bmatrix}e&f\\f&g\end{bmatrix}},}  {\displaystyle n={\frac {N}{|N|}}} is the unit normal to the surface at {\displaystyle r}, and because {\displaystyle {\frac {\partial r}{\partial \varphi }}} and {\displaystyle {\frac {\partial r}{\partial \lambda }}} are tangents to the surface,  {\displaystyle N={\frac {\partial r}{\partial \varphi }}\times {\frac {\partial r}{\partial \lambda }}}  is normal to the surface at {\displaystyle r}.  With {\displaystyle F=f=0} for an oblate spheroid, the curvatures are  {\displaystyle \kappa _{1}={\frac {g}{G}}} and {\displaystyle \kappa _{2}={\frac {e}{E}},}  and the principal radii of curvature are  {\displaystyle R_{1}={\frac {1}{\kappa _{1}}}} and {\displaystyle R_{2}={\frac {1}{\kappa _{2}}}.}  The first and second radii of curvature correspond, respectively, to the Earth's meridional and prime-vertical radii of curvature.  Geometrically, the second fundamental form gives the distance from {\displaystyle r+dr} to the plane tangent at {\displaystyle r}. |

##### Meridional

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_radius&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Meridional")]

In particular, the *Earth's [meridional](/wiki/Meridian_(geography) "Meridian (geography)") radius of curvature* (in the north–south direction) at φ is[[12]](#cite_note-Jekeli-17)

:   ![{\displaystyle M(\varphi )={\frac {(ab)^{2}}{{\big (}(a\cos \varphi )^{2}+(b\sin \varphi )^{2}{\big )}^{\frac {3}{2}}}}={\frac {a(1-e^{2})}{(1-e^{2}\sin ^{2}\varphi )^{\frac {3}{2}}}}={\frac {1-e^{2}}{a^{2}}}N(\varphi )^{3},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/24dd959e2ceb84ce9d1309242083ffd66d07c8a8)

where ![{\displaystyle e}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/cd253103f0876afc68ebead27a5aa9867d927467) is the [eccentricity](/wiki/Eccentricity_(mathematics) "Eccentricity (mathematics)") of the earth. This is the radius that [Eratosthenes](/wiki/Eratosthenes#Measurement_of_the_Earth "Eratosthenes") measured in his [arc measurement](/wiki/Arc_measurement "Arc measurement").

##### Prime vertical

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_radius&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Prime vertical")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Geodetic_latitude_and_the_length_of_Normal.svg/250px-Geodetic_latitude_and_the_length_of_Normal.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Geodetic_latitude_and_the_length_of_Normal.svg)

The length PQ, called the *prime vertical radius*, is ![{\displaystyle N(\phi ).}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ea7143e417ccafb66605e0e7dec2ed44f44d24c4) The length IQ is equal to ![{\displaystyle e^{2}N(\phi ).}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/dfe10a9c607882ff39c8247d40ff7ea0e3c070d8) ![{\displaystyle R=(X,Y,Z).}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/bad48e7a4ac7b28a418d7ddc99d0d970020b2866)

If one point had appeared due east of the other, one finds the approximate curvature in the east–west direction.[[f]](#cite_note-curvprim-18)
This *Earth's [prime-vertical](/wiki/Prime_vertical "Prime vertical") radius of curvature*, also called the *Earth's transverse radius of curvature*, is defined perpendicular ([orthogonal](/wiki/Orthogonal "Orthogonal")) to M at geodetic latitude φ[[g]](#cite_note-19) and is[[12]](#cite_note-Jekeli-17)

:   ![{\displaystyle N(\varphi )={\frac {a^{2}}{\sqrt {(a\cos \varphi )^{2}+(b\sin \varphi )^{2}}}}={\frac {a}{\sqrt {1-e^{2}\sin ^{2}\varphi }}}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a6d0f1832f82ce05d8d24ba1f3db1b8b195d39f7)

*N* can also be interpreted geometrically as the [normal distance](/wiki/Normal_distance "Normal distance") from the ellipsoid surface to the polar axis.[[13]](#cite_note-Bowring-20)
The radius of a [parallel of latitude](/wiki/Parallel_of_latitude "Parallel of latitude") is given by ![{\displaystyle p=N\cos(\varphi )}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a6dbc469e5b92e30b240a91a24dfef9461889569).[[14]](#cite_note-21)[[15]](#cite_note-22)

#### Combined radii of curvature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_radius&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Combined radii of curvature")]

##### Azimuthal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_radius&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Azimuthal")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Euler_radius_vs._azimuth_at_various_latitudes.svg/250px-Euler_radius_vs._azimuth_at_various_latitudes.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Euler_radius_vs._azimuth_at_various_latitudes.svg)

Euler radius vs. azimuth at various latitudes

**Earth's azimuthal radius of curvature** of an [Earth normal section](/wiki/Earth_normal_section "Earth normal section") at an [azimuth](/wiki/Azimuth "Azimuth") (measured clockwise from north) α and at latitude φ, is derived from [Euler's curvature formula](/wiki/Euler%27s_theorem_(differential_geometry) "Euler's theorem (differential geometry)") as follows:[[16]](#cite_note-Torge-23): 97

:   ![{\displaystyle R_{\mathrm {c} }={\frac {1}{{\dfrac {\cos ^{2}\alpha }{M}}+{\dfrac {\sin ^{2}\alpha }{N}}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9f5d70f36f379328650cf3f61485e2ace6dc2b64)

or

:   ![{\displaystyle R_{\mathrm {c} }^{-1}=M^{-1}\cos ^{2}\alpha +N^{-1}\sin ^{2}\alpha ,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f11127397ffd7fc0312916d4051d1ccdfe5aea7d)

where ![{\displaystyle R_{\mathrm {c} }^{-1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1e6755b2c323384e1cde1beb44d6cae9fce8327f) is the azimuthal curvature of the ellipsoid.

##### Non-directional (Gaussian and mean)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_radius&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Non-directional (Gaussian and mean)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Difference_between_mean_and_Gaussian_radii_of_curvature.svg/250px-Difference_between_mean_and_Gaussian_radii_of_curvature.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Difference_between_mean_and_Gaussian_radii_of_curvature.svg)

Difference between mean and Gaussian radii of curvature

It is possible to combine the principal radii of curvature above in a non-directional manner.

**Earth's Gaussian radius of curvature** at latitude φ is[[16]](#cite_note-Torge-23)

:   ![{\displaystyle R_{\text{a}}(\varphi )={\frac {1}{\sqrt {K}}}={\frac {1}{2\pi }}\int _{0}^{2\pi }R_{\text{c}}(\alpha )\,d\alpha ,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/bee9b523895a92963ec77433206900d86361a8e3)

or

:   ![{\displaystyle R_{\text{a}}(\varphi )={\sqrt {MN}}={\frac {a^{2}b}{(a\cos \varphi )^{2}+(b\sin \varphi )^{2}}}={\frac {a{\sqrt {1-e^{2}}}}{1-e^{2}\sin ^{2}\varphi }},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7fdef38c633dcb3e8a79af242e1b65ccc7c70280)

where *K* is the *[Gaussian curvature](/wiki/Gaussian_curvature "Gaussian curvature")*, ![{\displaystyle K=\kappa _{1}\,\kappa _{2}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2cbbbf0d5da73cd3ad3f0f0e2a81bf3bcc90900f)
The [Gaussian radius of curvature](/wiki/Gaussian_radius_of_curvature "Gaussian radius of curvature") is defined in terms of the product of the principal radii of curvature and it integrates the azimuthal radius of curvature ![{\displaystyle R_{\mathrm {c} }}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4f7e797851d12bf345b126b635fee1abf36bc0d6) over the full circle.
It corresponds to the radius of the *[osculating sphere](/wiki/Osculating_sphere "Osculating sphere")* that best fits the ellipsoid locally.

**Earth's radius of mean curvature** at latitude φ is[[16]](#cite_note-Torge-23): 97

:   ![{\displaystyle R_{\text{m}}={\frac {2}{{\dfrac {1}{M}}+{\dfrac {1}{N}}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c25b6fc7b6339ff5566e4d13d6f922554a047d39)

or

:   ![{\displaystyle R_{\text{m}}^{-1}={\frac {M^{-1}+N^{-1}}{2}}={\frac {1}{2\pi }}\int _{0}^{2\pi }R_{c}^{-1}(\theta )\;d\theta .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/03b61477d857c9bba476132e3cebc4f89a0c803e)

The [mean curvature](/wiki/Mean_curvature "Mean curvature") ![{\displaystyle R_{\text{m}}^{-1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/13cf369710fe544cf0b30eb87d74babf18f6339c) equals the arithmetic mean of the two principal curvatures and integrates the azimuthal curvature ![{\displaystyle R_{\mathrm {c} }^{-1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1e6755b2c323384e1cde1beb44d6cae9fce8327f) over the full circle.

##### Equatorial radii of curvature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_radius&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Equatorial radii of curvature")]

*Earth's meridional radius of curvature at the equator* equals the meridian's [semi-latus rectum](/wiki/Semi-latus_rectum "Semi-latus rectum"):

:   ![{\displaystyle M(0{\text{°}})={\frac {b^{2}}{a}}=\ell =6,335.439{\text{ km.}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/cffa106b6eb9592af6e44a3dbc396fe2302b2de9)

*Earth's prime-vertical radius of curvature at the equator* equals the equatorial radius,

:   ![{\displaystyle N(0{\text{°}})=a}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/bcc5d90bdedb0c61b7423d98d82769c451d0a35c)

*Earth's Gaussian radius of curvature at the equator* simplifies to the polar radius (*not* the equatorial radius):

:   ![{\displaystyle R_{\text{a}}(0{\text{°}})=b}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ed966ab311f007db9fda1325ff71a23804e3057e)

*Earth's mean radius of curvature at the equator* involves the semi-latus rectum:

:   ![{\displaystyle R_{\text{m}}(0{\text{°}})=2(a\cdot \ell )/(a+\ell )=6,356.716{\text{ km.}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6d2292a7d5e577c7c258e2b87c01d753857073e7)

##### Polar radii of curvature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_radius&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Polar radii of curvature")]

*Earth's radius of curvature at the poles* (either meridional or prime-vertical) is

:   ![{\displaystyle M(90{\text{°}})=N(90{\text{°}})={\frac {a^{2}}{b}}=6,399.594{\text{ km.}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7af7cf04bad537f7b9f40a432b9cb4fd0a5c8eb2)

*Earth's Gaussian or mean radii of curvature at the poles* also equal the principal radii of curvature at the poles (*not* the polar radius):

:   ![{\displaystyle R_{\text{a}}(90{\text{°}})=R_{\text{m}}(90{\text{°}})={\frac {a^{2}}{b}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/75b3def4469538c451f7a8216c919d95e88c38d5)

## Global radii

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_radius&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Global radii")]

The Earth can be modeled as a sphere in many ways. This section describes the common ways. The various radii derived here use the notation and dimensions noted above for the Earth as derived from the [WGS-84](/wiki/WGS-84 "WGS-84") ellipsoid;[[6]](#cite_note-tr8350_2-11) namely,

:   *Equatorial radius*: a = (6378.1370 km)
:   *Polar radius*: b = (6356.7523 km)

A sphere being a gross approximation of the spheroid, which itself is an approximation of the geoid, units are given here in kilometers rather than the millimeter resolution appropriate for geodesy.

### Arithmetic mean radius

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_radius&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Arithmetic mean radius")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/WGS84_mean_Earth_radius.svg/250px-WGS84_mean_Earth_radius.svg.png)](/wiki/File:WGS84_mean_Earth_radius.svg)

Equatorial (*a*), polar (*b*) and arithmetic mean Earth radii as defined in the 1984 [World Geodetic System](/wiki/World_Geodetic_System "World Geodetic System") revision (not to scale)

In geophysics, the [International Union of Geodesy and Geophysics](/wiki/International_Union_of_Geodesy_and_Geophysics "International Union of Geodesy and Geophysics") (IUGG) defines the *Earth's [arithmetic mean](/wiki/Arithmetic_mean "Arithmetic mean") radius* (denoted *R*1) to be[[2]](#cite_note-Moritz-2)

:   ![{\displaystyle R_{1}={\frac {2a+b}{3}}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2d6a99d0ae6eebbb7bf5ab80042eb12141994bb1)

The factor of two accounts for the biaxial symmetry in Earth's spheroid, a specialization of triaxial ellipsoid.[[17]](#cite_note-Moritz2000-24)
For Earth, the arithmetic mean radius is published by IUGG and [NGA](/wiki/National_Geospatial-Intelligence_Agency "National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency") as 6,371.0087714 km (3,958.7613160 mi).[[1]](#cite_note-IAU_XXIX-1)[[7]](#cite_note-NGA-12)

### Authalic radius

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_radius&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Authalic radius")]

See also: [Authalic latitude](/wiki/Authalic_latitude "Authalic latitude")

*Earth's authalic radius* (meaning ["equal area"](/wiki/Equal-area_projection "Equal-area projection")) is the radius of a hypothetical perfect sphere that has the same surface area as the [reference ellipsoid](/wiki/Reference_ellipsoid "Reference ellipsoid"). The [IUGG](/wiki/IUGG "IUGG") denotes the authalic radius as *R*2.[[2]](#cite_note-Moritz-2)
A closed-form solution exists for a spheroid:[[8]](#cite_note-Snyder_manual-13)

:   ![{\displaystyle R_{2}={\sqrt {{\frac {1}{2}}\left(a^{2}+{\frac {b^{2}}{e}}\ln {\frac {1+e}{b/a}}\right)}}={\sqrt {{\frac {a^{2}}{2}}+{\frac {b^{2}}{2}}{\frac {\tanh ^{-1}e}{e}}}}={\sqrt {\frac {A}{4\pi }}},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c7358c01700ed23568d45ac2cd1febd05dd0d91c)

where ⁠![{\displaystyle \textstyle e={\sqrt {a^{2}-b^{2}}}{\big /}a}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ba264a415e730e2122a2e0d366f8d967a44fc9b5)⁠ is the eccentricity, and ⁠![{\displaystyle A}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7daff47fa58cdfd29dc333def748ff5fa4c923e3)⁠ is the surface area of the spheroid.

For the Earth, the authalic radius is 6,371.0072 km (3,958.7603 mi).[[17]](#cite_note-Moritz2000-24)

The authalic radius ![{\displaystyle R_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/35f571121c264178676d1df8ab899f238a39bc2c) also corresponds to the *radius of (global) mean curvature*, obtained by averaging the Gaussian curvature, ![{\displaystyle K}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2b76fce82a62ed5461908f0dc8f037de4e3686b0), over the surface of the ellipsoid. Using the [Gauss–Bonnet theorem](/wiki/Gauss%E2%80%93Bonnet_theorem "Gauss–Bonnet theorem"), this gives

:   ![{\displaystyle {\frac {\int K\,dA}{A}}={\frac {4\pi }{A}}={\frac {1}{R_{2}^{2}}}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/60a3a58a72cf1f1c688a12ffe3cc3de290a47804)

### Volumetric radius

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_radius&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Volumetric radius")]

Another spherical model is defined by the *Earth's volumetric radius*, which is the radius of a sphere of volume equal to the ellipsoid. The [IUGG](/wiki/IUGG "IUGG") denotes the volumetric radius as *R*3.[[2]](#cite_note-Moritz-2)

:   ![{\displaystyle R_{3}={\sqrt[{3}]{a^{2}b}}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/70c304c12d43772df15ea6ea3be8e835d270b47b)

For Earth, the volumetric radius equals 6,371.0008 km (3,958.7564 mi).[[17]](#cite_note-Moritz2000-24)

### Rectifying radius

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_radius&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Rectifying radius")]

See also: [Quarter meridian](/wiki/Quarter_meridian "Quarter meridian") and [Rectifying latitude](/wiki/Rectifying_latitude "Rectifying latitude")

Another global radius is the *Earth's rectifying radius*, giving a sphere with circumference equal to the [perimeter](/wiki/Circumference "Circumference") of the ellipse described by any polar cross section of the ellipsoid. This requires an [elliptic integral](/wiki/Circumference#Ellipse "Circumference") to find, given the polar and equatorial radii:

:   ![{\displaystyle M_{\text{r}}={\frac {2}{\pi }}\int _{0}^{\frac {\pi }{2}}{\sqrt {a^{2}\cos ^{2}\varphi +b^{2}\sin ^{2}\varphi }}\,d\varphi .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9da3dfc49d996dc1c07bcee019ba1bdaf6e3f2e1)

The rectifying radius is equivalent to the meridional mean, which is defined as the average value of M:[[8]](#cite_note-Snyder_manual-13)

:   ![{\displaystyle M_{\text{r}}={\frac {2}{\pi }}\int _{0}^{\frac {\pi }{2}}M(\varphi )\,d\varphi .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f0dcd6b9d2274a2951c9751e5452fba972a4955a)

For integration limits of [0,⁠π/2⁠], the integrals for rectifying radius and mean radius evaluate to the same result, which, for Earth, amounts to 6,367.4491 km (3,956.5494 mi).

The meridional mean is well approximated by the semicubic mean of the two axes,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

:   ![{\displaystyle M_{\text{r}}\approx \left({\frac {a^{\frac {3}{2}}+b^{\frac {3}{2}}}{2}}\right)^{\frac {2}{3}},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/3b5a7a312adf176d07c8d45343bc3ec270110322)

which differs from the exact result by less than 1 μm (4×10−5 in); the mean of the two axes,

:   ![{\displaystyle M_{\text{r}}\approx {\frac {a+b}{2}},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e1d76b66cd56d5a496970b910d5ad7fe409a4a43)

about 6,367.445 km (3,956.547 mi), can also be used.

## Topographical radii

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_radius&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Topographical radii")]

See also: [Earth § Size and shape](/wiki/Earth#Size_and_shape "Earth")

The mathematical expressions above apply over the surface of the ellipsoid.
The cases below considers Earth's [topography](/wiki/Topography "Topography"), above or below a [reference ellipsoid](/wiki/Reference_ellipsoid "Reference ellipsoid").
As such, they are *topographical [geocentric distances](/wiki/Geocentric_distance "Geocentric distance")*, *R*t, which depends not only on latitude.

### Topographical extremes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_radius&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Topographical extremes")]

* Maximum *R*t: the summit of [Chimborazo](/wiki/Chimborazo "Chimborazo") is 6,384.4 km (3,967.1 mi) from the Earth's center.
* Minimum *R*t: the floor of the [Arctic Ocean](/wiki/Arctic_Ocean "Arctic Ocean") is 6,352.8 km (3,947.4 mi) from the Earth's center.[[18]](#cite_note-extrema-25)

### Topographical global mean

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_radius&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Topographical global mean")]

The *topographical mean geocentric distance* averages elevations everywhere, resulting in a value 230 m larger than the [IUGG mean radius](#Arithmetic_mean_radius), the [authalic radius](/wiki/Authalic_radius "Authalic radius"), or the [volumetric radius](#Volumetric_radius). This topographical average is 6,371.230 km (3,958.899 mi) with uncertainty of 10 m (33 ft).[[19]](#cite_note-chambat-26)

## Derived quantities: diameter, circumference, arc-length, area, volume

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_radius&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Derived quantities: diameter, circumference, arc-length, area, volume")]

*Earth's [diameter](/wiki/Diameter "Diameter")* is simply twice Earth's radius; for example, *equatorial diameter* (2*a*) and *polar diameter* (2*b*). For the WGS84 ellipsoid, that's respectively:

:   2*a* = 12,756.2740 km (7,926.3812 mi),
:   2*b* = 12,713.5046 km (7,899.8055 mi).

*[Earth's circumference](/wiki/Earth%27s_circumference "Earth's circumference")* equals the [perimeter](/wiki/Perimeter "Perimeter") length. The *equatorial circumference* is simply the [circle perimeter](/wiki/Circle_perimeter "Circle perimeter"): *C*e = 2*πa*, in terms of the equatorial radius *a*:

:   *C*e = 40,075.0167 km (24,901.461 mi)

The *polar circumference* equals *C*p = 4*m*p, four times the [quarter meridian](/wiki/Quarter_meridian "Quarter meridian") *m*p = *aE*(*e*):

:   *C*p = 40,007.8629173 km (24,859.733 mi)

The polar radius *b* enters via the eccentricity *e* = (1 − *b*2/*a*2)0.5; see [Ellipse#Circumference](/wiki/Ellipse#Circumference "Ellipse") for details.

[Arc length](/wiki/Arc_length "Arc length") of more general [surface curves](/wiki/Surface_curve "Surface curve"), such as [meridian arcs](/wiki/Meridian_arc "Meridian arc") and [geodesics](/wiki/Earth_geodesics "Earth geodesics"), can also be derived from Earth's equatorial and polar radii.

Likewise for [surface area](/wiki/Surface_area "Surface area"), either based on a [map projection](/wiki/Map_projection "Map projection") or a [geodesic polygon](/wiki/Geodesic_polygon "Geodesic polygon").

**Earth's volume**, or that of the reference ellipsoid, is ![{\displaystyle V={\tfrac {4}{3}}\pi a^{2}b.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7d6d9aca4d371f255c1f6cb74122bccf21c214d4) Using the parameters from [WGS84](/wiki/WGS84 "WGS84") ellipsoid of revolution, *a* = 6,378.137 km and *b* = 6356.7523142 km, it results in:[[20]](#cite_note-earth_fact_sheet-27)

:   *V* = 1.08321×1012 km3 (2.5988×1011 mi3)

## Nominal radii

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_radius&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Nominal radii")]

In astronomy, the [International Astronomical Union](/wiki/International_Astronomical_Union "International Astronomical Union") denotes the *nominal equatorial Earth radius* as ![{\displaystyle {\mathcal {R}}_{\text{eE}}^{\text{N}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ea9ce43d9005404d3654534daa978785e414155f), which is defined to be exactly 6,378.1 km (3,963.2 mi).[[1]](#cite_note-IAU_XXIX-1): 3 The *nominal polar Earth radius* is defined exactly as ![{\displaystyle {\mathcal {R}}_{\text{pE}}^{\text{N}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e22d9e5db5a1cd92b415f722945585c768390675) = 6,356.8 km (3,949.9 mi). These values correspond to the zero [Earth tide](/wiki/Earth_tide "Earth tide") convention. Equatorial radius is conventionally used as the nominal value unless the polar radius is explicitly required.[[1]](#cite_note-IAU_XXIX-1): 4
The nominal radius serves as a [unit of length](/wiki/Unit_of_length "Unit of length") for [astronomy](/wiki/Astronomical_system_of_units "Astronomical system of units").
(The notation is defined such that it can be easily generalized for other [planets](/wiki/Planet#Size_and_shape "Planet"); e.g., ![{\displaystyle {\mathcal {R}}_{\text{pJ}}^{\text{N}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/41295f294f64ffbaea092d83a9eb88009d8f1dde) for the nominal polar [Jupiter radius](/wiki/Jupiter_radius "Jupiter radius").)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_radius&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [History of geodesy](/wiki/History_of_geodesy "History of geodesy"), [Spherical Earth § History](/wiki/Spherical_Earth#History "Spherical Earth"), [Earth's circumference § History](/wiki/Earth%27s_circumference#History "Earth's circumference"), and [Meridian arc § History](/wiki/Meridian_arc#History "Meridian arc")

The first published reference to the Earth's size appeared around 350 [BC](/wiki/Anno_Domini "Anno Domini"), when [Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle") reported in his book *[On the Heavens](/wiki/On_the_Heavens "On the Heavens")*[[22]](#cite_note-29) that mathematicians had guessed the circumference of the Earth to be 400,000 [stadia](/wiki/Stadia_(length) "Stadia (length)"). Scholars have interpreted Aristotle's figure to be anywhere from highly accurate[[23]](#cite_note-30) to almost double the true value.[[24]](#cite_note-31) The first known scientific measurement and calculation of the circumference of the Earth was performed by [Eratosthenes](/wiki/Eratosthenes "Eratosthenes") in about 240 BC. Estimates of the error of Eratosthenes's measurement range from 0.5% to 17%.[[25]](#cite_note-32) For both Aristotle and Eratosthenes, uncertainty in the accuracy of their estimates is due to modern uncertainty over which stadion length they meant.

Around 100 BC, [Posidonius of Apamea](/wiki/Posidonius_of_Apamea "Posidonius of Apamea") recomputed Earth's radius, and found it to be close to that by Eratosthenes,[[26]](#cite_note-33) but later [Strabo](/wiki/Strabo "Strabo") incorrectly attributed him a value about 3/4 of the actual size.[[27]](#cite_note-34) [Claudius Ptolemy](/wiki/Claudius_Ptolemy "Claudius Ptolemy") around 150 [AD](/wiki/Anno_Domini "Anno Domini") gave empirical evidence supporting a [spherical Earth](/wiki/Spherical_Earth "Spherical Earth"),[[28]](#cite_note-35) but he accepted the lesser value attributed to Posidonius. His highly influential work, the *[Almagest](/wiki/Almagest "Almagest")*,[[29]](#cite_note-36) left no doubt among medieval scholars that Earth is spherical, but they were wrong about its size.

By 1490, [Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus") believed that traveling 3,000 miles west from the west coast of the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula") would let him reach the eastern coasts of [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia").[[30]](#cite_note-37) However, the 1492 enactment of that voyage [brought his fleet to the Americas](/wiki/Voyages_of_Christopher_Columbus "Voyages of Christopher Columbus"). The [Magellan expedition](/wiki/Magellan_expedition "Magellan expedition") (1519–1522), which was the first [circumnavigation](/wiki/Circumnavigation "Circumnavigation") of the World, soundly demonstrated the sphericity of the Earth,[[31]](#cite_note-38) and affirmed the original measurement of 40,000 km (25,000 mi) by Eratosthenes.

Around 1690, [Isaac Newton](/wiki/Isaac_Newton "Isaac Newton") and [Christiaan Huygens](/wiki/Christiaan_Huygens "Christiaan Huygens") argued that Earth was closer to an [oblate spheroid](/wiki/Spheroid#Oblate_spheroids "Spheroid") than to a sphere. However, around 1730, [Jacques Cassini](/wiki/Jacques_Cassini "Jacques Cassini") argued for a [prolate spheroid](/wiki/Spheroid#Prolate_spheroids "Spheroid") instead, due to different interpretations of the [Newtonian mechanics](/wiki/Newtonian_mechanics "Newtonian mechanics") involved.[[32]](#cite_note-39) To settle the matter, the [French Geodesic Mission](/wiki/French_Geodesic_Mission "French Geodesic Mission") (1735–1739) measured one degree of [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude") at two locations, one near the [Arctic Circle](/wiki/Arctic_Circle "Arctic Circle") and the other near the [equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator"). The expedition found that Newton's conjecture was correct:[[33]](#cite_note-40) the Earth is flattened at the [poles](/wiki/Geographical_pole "Geographical pole") due to rotation's [centrifugal force](/wiki/Centrifugal_force "Centrifugal force").

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth_radius&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-3)** For details see [figure of the Earth](/wiki/Figure_of_the_Earth "Figure of the Earth"), [geoid](/wiki/Geoid "Geoid"), and [Earth tide](/wiki/Earth_tide "Earth tide").
2. **[^](#cite_ref-4)** There is no single center to the geoid; it varies according to local [geodetic](/wiki/Geodetic_system "Geodetic system") conditions. Where there is land, the geoid is generally below ground; it represents where the sea level would be if water could reach it from the ocean via an imaginary canal.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-5)** In a geocentric ellipsoid, the center of the ellipsoid coincides with some computed center of Earth, and best models the earth as a whole. Geodetic ellipsoids are better suited to regional idiosyncrasies of the geoid. A partial surface of an ellipsoid gets fitted to the region, in which case the center and orientation of the ellipsoid generally do not coincide with the earth's center of mass or axis of rotation.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-6)** The value of the radius is completely dependent upon the latitude in the case of an ellipsoid model, and nearly so on the geoid.
5. **[^](#cite_ref-8)** This follows from the [International Astronomical Union](/wiki/International_Astronomical_Union "International Astronomical Union") [definition](/wiki/2006_definition_of_planet "2006 definition of planet") rule (2): a planet assumes a shape due to [hydrostatic equilibrium](/wiki/Hydrostatic_equilibrium "Hydrostatic equilibrium") where [gravity](/wiki/Gravity "Gravity") and [centrifugal forces](/wiki/Centrifugal_force "Centrifugal force") are nearly balanced.[[3]](#cite_note-7)
6. **[^](#cite_ref-curvprim_18-0)** East–west directions can be misleading. Point B, which appears due east from A, will be closer to the equator than A. Thus the curvature found this way is smaller than the curvature of a circle of constant latitude, except at the equator. West can be exchanged for east in this discussion.
7. **[^](#cite_ref-19)** N is defined as the radius of curvature in the plane that is normal to both the surface of the ellipsoid at, and the meridian passing through, the specific point of interest.
